Ajinkya Rahane is a renowned Indian cricketer and former captain of the Indian cricket team. Born on June 6, 1988, Rahane has been a significant presence in the cricketing world since his debut in 2007. He has played for the Indian national team in various formats, including Test matches, One Day Internationals (ODIs), and Twenty20 Internationals (T20Is). Rahane's IPL journey began in 2008 with Rajasthan Royals, where he spent most of his prime years. He later played for Rising Pune Supergiants, Chennai Super Kings, and currently, Kolkata Knight Riders (KKR), whom he captains. As an accomplished batter, Rahane has scored over 2,500 runs for Rajasthan Royals between 2011 and 2019. His standout season came in 2012 when he scored 560 runs, including a 103* against RCB. One of Rahane's notable achievements is his impressive record as a batter in international cricket. He has scored over 7,000 runs in international cricket and has been a crucial player for India in various series. Rahane led India to a historic Border-Gavaskar Trophy triumph in Australia in 2020/21, showcasing his leadership skills. Rahane has received several accolades for his contributions to Indian cricket. He was awarded the Arjuna Award in 2016 for his stellar performance in the sport. As a batter, Rahane is known for his consistency and ability to score runs in challenging conditions. He is one of the few players to have scored 1,000 runs in a single Ranji season, achieving this feat in 2009. As captain, Rahane has led teams in various formats, including the IPL. He has captained KKR since 2024 and has a record of 14 wins in 43 IPL matches as captain, although his win percentage is one of the lowest in IPL history. Despite this, Rahane has shown his ability to perform under pressure, scoring runs consistently for his team. Throughout his career, Rahane has been part of several notable partnerships and has played a crucial role in various series. He has been a part of the IPL since its inception and has achieved significant milestones, including becoming the 9th player to score 5,000 runs in the tournament. Rahane's contributions to Indian cricket, both as a player and a leader, have made him a respected figure in the cricketing world.

Umesh Yadav ( born 25 October 1987) is a right‑arm fast bowler who has represented India in all three formats and is best known for his role as a death‑over specialist in the Indian Premier League. A native of Vidarbha, Yadav broke into international cricket in 2010 and was a member of India’s 2013 ICC Champions Trophy‑winning side. In the IPL he first appeared for Royal Challengers Bangalore in 2013, finishing the season with 11 wickets and helping the franchise reach the playoffs. The following year he moved to Kolkata Knight Riders and was part of the KKR squad that lifted the IPL trophy in 2014, contributing crucial breakthroughs throughout the campaign. Yadav’s IPL journey continued with stints at Chennai Super Kings (2019) and Delhi Capitals (2021), before returning to KKR ahead of the 2022 season. His four‑wicket haul of 4/23 against Punjab Kings in March 2022 propelled him to the top of the wicket‑taking charts and earned him the Purple Cap after just three matches, marking the first time he held the league’s leading‑bowler award. Over his IPL career he has featured in more than 120 matches and taken close to 120 wickets, maintaining an economy rate in the high‑eight to low‑nine runs‑per‑over range and a bowling average around the low‑thirties. Renowned within the Indian dressing room as the “Strongman” for his robust physique and ability to generate pace in the high‑140 km/h band while still swinging the ball, Yadav is routinely deployed in the final overs to restrict scoring and take wickets. His consistency in the death overs, combined with occasional breakthroughs in the powerplay, has made him a valuable asset for every franchise he has served. Off the field he is recognised for his disciplined fitness regimen and his role in mentoring younger pacers in the KKR camp, reinforcing his status as a distinguished Indian fast‑bowling figure in modern T20 cricket.

Rasikh Salam Dar, born 5 April 2000 in Jammu and Kashmir, is an Indian right‑handed medium‑pace bowler known for his lethal death‑over deliveries. He made his List A debut for Jammu & Kashmir in the 2018–19 Vijay Hazare Trophy and his first‑class debut in the Ranji Trophy a few weeks later, before breaking into the Syed Mushtaq Ali Trophy in February 2019. His performances earned him a place in the Mumbai Indians squad for the 2019 IPL, where he made a brief but memorable debut, taking 1/23 on debut at Wankhede. After a two‑year suspension imposed by the BCCI over discrepancies in his birth certificate and a serious lower‑back injury that curtailed his 2019 season, Salam returned to form in domestic cricket and was signed by Delhi for the 2024 IPL season. In 2024, he was named in India’s Emerging T20 Asia Cup squad in Oman, emerging as the tournament’s leading wicket‑taker and earning praise for his ability to swing the ball at the death. Following the IPL, he moved to Baroda for the 2025–26 domestic season, though he remained on the RCB roster for the 2026 IPL, where he delivered a match‑winning spell of 3/34 in a high‑pressure fixture. In the 2026 season, he became the highest‑economical bowler in the league with an average of 10.29 runs per over, underscoring his proficiency in restricting runs. Salam’s IPL statistics to date include 16 matches, 48 overs, 14 wickets at an average of 35.29 and an economy of 10.29. He has never taken a five‑for in the league but has consistently claimed wickets in the final overs, earning him the reputation of a “death bowler”. His journey from the hills of Jammu and Kashmir to the bright lights of the IPL is frequently cited as an inspirational narrative within Indian cricket. Despite a modest price tier of under one crore in the IPL auctions, Salam’s impact on the field and his resilience off it have cemented his place as a noteworthy figure in contemporary Indian T20 cricket.

Anukul Roy is an Indian cricketer who has made a name for himself as a left-arm spin bowling all-rounder. Born on November 30, 1998, in Seraikela Kharsawan, Jharkhand, Roy rose to prominence with his impressive performance in the 2018 ICC Men's Under-19 World Cup, where he was the joint-highest wicket-taker in India's title-winning campaign, claiming 14 wickets with his left-arm spin. Roy's IPL career began when he was drafted by the Mumbai Indians in 2018 for INR 20 lakh. Although opportunities were limited, and he featured in just one game across four seasons, he made his presence felt in the field and was regularly used by Mumbai as a substitute fielder. In 2022, Kolkata Knight Riders secured him for INR 20 lakh at the auction, and he has remained with the franchise since. In the 2022 Syed Mushtaq Ali Trophy, Roy had a standout campaign, emerging as the third-highest run-scorer. He also played a key role in Jharkhand's first-ever Syed Mushtaq Ali Trophy triumph, scoring 303 runs and picking up 18 wickets, including an unbeaten 40 off 20 and a couple of wickets in the final, which helped him earn the Player of the Tournament award. Roy has played 22 matches to date in his IPL career and has scored 65 runs, with an average of 9.29. His highest IPL score is 29* runs. As a bowling all-rounder, Roy has proven to be an invaluable asset in domestic cricket, representing Jharkhand in the Ranji Trophy and in various formats. Anukul Roy is a proper left-arm spinner, knowing how to use his variations to his advantage. He is often compared to players like Jadeja and Axar due to his skills. Roy bowls stump-to-stump and is a gun fielder. With Kolkata Knight Riders, he was part of the franchise's title-winning campaign in 2024. Throughout his career, Roy has demonstrated his potential as a spin bowling all-rounder, and his role will be crucial in the team's setup. He continues to solidify his spot in KKR's lineup, showcasing his skills on the big stage.

K. L. Rahul, born 18 April 1992 in Bangalore, is a right‑handed Indian wicket‑keeper‑batter who has become one of the most recognizable figures in modern Twenty‑20 cricket. Making his IPL debut for Royal Challengers Bangalore in 2013, he has represented five franchises – RCB (2013, 2016), Sunrisers Hyderabad, Delhi Capitals and, most recently, Lucknow Super Giants (LSG), where he serves as captain and primary keeper. His tenure with Delhi Capitals produced the bulk of his run‑scoring, and in the 2026 season he topped the Orange Cap chart with 445 runs in ten matches, posting an average of 49.44 and a strike‑rate above 180. The innings of 152 * — the first 150‑plus score by an Indian in IPL history — cemented his status as a big‑hitter capable of changing a game in a single spell. Rahul’s consistency is reflected in his record as the fastest player to reach 5 000 IPL runs and his accumulation of fifteen Player‑of‑the‑Match awards, the most any player has earned since 2018. By surpassing MS Dhoni he entered the league’s top five run‑getters, a milestone that underscores his longevity and adaptability across batting positions. As a captain, he has led Punjab Kings in 2020 and LSG in 2024‑26, amassing 42 IPL matches as skipper with a win‑loss‑tie record of 20‑20‑2 and a personal batting average of 56.23, including fifteen half‑centuries and three centuries. Internationally, Rahul is a regular member of India’s limited‑overs squads, featuring in T20I fixtures and contributing as a flexible top‑order option and reliable keeper. His dual role as a wicket‑keeper and a high‑impact batter distinguishes him from specialist keepers and pure batsmen, offering teams balance in squad composition. Off the field, he is noted for his measured demeanor, often emphasizing team titles over individual milestones, a viewpoint that aligns with his pursuit of multiple IPL championships. Collectively, K. L. Rahul’s statistical achievements, leadership roles, and record‑setting innings have etched his name firmly into the annals of IPL and Indian cricket history.

Quinton de Kock is a South African cricketer who has made a lasting impact on the sport. Born in Cape Town, South Africa, de Kock has been an integral part of the South African cricket team across all three formats. He is widely regarded as a skilled wicket-keeper and a talented batsman, known for his exceptional hand-eye coordination and ability to find the gaps effortlessly. De Kock's IPL legacy is deeply intertwined with the Mumbai Indians, where he played for three consecutive seasons from 2019 to 2021, helping the team secure back-to-back titles in 2019 and 2020. Ayush Badoni (born 3 December 1999) is an Indian left‑handed all‑rounder who has made his mark in the Indian Premier League with the Lucknow Super Giants (LSG). Making his IPL debut in 2022, Badoni instantly became the first player to score a half‑century on debut while batting at number six or lower, a feat that highlighted his ability to anchor lower‑order innings and accelerate when required. Over the course of the 2025 season he compiled 329 runs from 14 matches at an impressive strike rate of 148.20, with a career‑best score of 74 against Punjab Kings. Since his entry into the IPL, Badoni has played 64 matches for LSG, amassing 1,135 runs at an average of 25.80, including seven half‑centuries. His performances earned him a significant salary increase, with the franchise retaining him at ₹4 Crore ahead of the 2026 season after a ₹4 Crore jump in the 2025 mega auction. Despite being a budget‑tier player (<₹1 Crore) at the time of his acquisition, Badoni’s consistency has made him a core finisher for the Super Giants. In domestic cricket, Badoni represents Delhi and is known for his occasional off‑spin bowling. He impressed at the U‑19 level with a 185‑run knock against Sri Lanka, complemented by four wickets for 24 runs, underscoring his all‑round capabilities. His first‑class record includes 675 runs in six innings, further evidencing his proficiency with the bat in longer formats. While Badoni has not yet featured in a T20I match, media reports have noted him as a potential India squad member ahead of the 2026 T20 World Cup, reflecting his rising profile within Indian cricket circles. His journey from a domestic talent to a reliable IPL finisher illustrates the growing depth of Indian all‑rounders, and his continued development keeps him on the radar of national selectors and franchise strategists alike.

Suryakumar Yadav, born in Mumbai, is a right‑handed international cricketer who has become one of the most influential figures in the modern white‑ball format. A product of the Mumbai cricketing system, he made his T20I debut for India in March 2021 and followed it up with an ODI debut in July of the same year. In November 2023 he was appointed captain of the Indian T20I side, a role he retained after Rohit Sharma’s retirement in 2024. Under his leadership India won the 2024 ICC T20 World Cup and the 2025 Asia Cup, achieving a 79.54 % win rate in 22 matches. Yadav’s domestic career is inseparable from the Mumbai Indians franchise. He joined MI in 2011, played a pivotal role in the club’s 2011 Champions League T20 triumph, and has been an ever‑present figure in the team’s five IPL title victories (2013, 2015, 2017, 2019, 2020). His aggressive, big‑hitting style has redefined the middle‑order for MI, producing the most runs in an IPL season (717 in 2025) and the most sixes in a season (38). He holds the record for 25+ runs in 11 innings of a single IPL edition and was the Orange Cap holder in 2025, surpassing Virat Kohli and Sai Sudharsan. Internationally, Yadav has amassed more than 3,000 runs across formats, including four centuries, and maintained the ICC T20I top ranking from October 2022 to June 2024. He has been named ICC T20I Player of the Year in consecutive years (2022, 2023) and has delivered match‑winning knocks such as an unbeaten 103 off 49 balls against Gujarat Titans in 2023. His IPL statistics feature 30 fifties and a career‑high 103*, underscoring his consistency and power. Yadav’s legacy lies in his ability to combine explosive batting with a calm, methodical approach, making him a cornerstone of both MI’s sustained success and India’s white‑ball dominance. His achievements, from franchise glory to national triumphs, cement his place as a pivotal figure in contemporary cricket history.
